Entity_Description: Bowen Island is an island municipality in Howe Sound, part of Metro Vancouver, British Columbia, with a population of 4,256 (2021 Census). It is the only island municipality in BC, incorporated in 1999, and lies about 19 km from downtown Vancouver, reached by BC Ferries from Horseshoe Bay to Snug Cove. Once a famed Union Steamship Company resort destination in the early 1900s, the island today mixes heritage cottages with modern custom homes across forested slopes rising to Mount Gardner, its highest peak.

Long_Description:
Bowen Island runs on the ferry schedule. The 20-minute BC Ferries crossing from Horseshoe Bay to Snug Cove shapes daily life for the island's 4,256 residents — and it shapes how {SERVICE} gets done here. A crew that misses a sailing loses hours, and a crew that forgets a part loses the whole day. {COMPANY_NAME} builds every Bowen Island job around that reality: materials confirmed before departure, sailings booked into the work plan, and no return trips your invoice has to absorb.

The island itself rewards that kind of preparation. As British Columbia's only island municipality — incorporated in 1999 — Bowen packs remarkable variety into its forested terrain. Snug Cove is the working heart, where the ferry dock, shops, and Crippen Regional Park's trails to Killarney Lake converge. Artisan Square adds studios and small businesses on the hillside above. Beyond the village, homes scatter along winding rural roads, many on well and septic, tucked under tall second-growth forest that climbs toward Mount Gardner, the island's highest point.

That building stock tells the island's story. Bowen was a celebrated Union Steamship Company resort in the early twentieth century, and cottages from that era still stand alongside 1970s-and-later homes and substantial modern builds owned by commuters and remote workers who ride the ferry to Vancouver, just 19 km away. Each vintage brings its own maintenance profile, and Howe Sound's climate tests all of them: heavy November-through-March rainfall drives drainage, moss, and moisture problems; salt-laden air works on exterior finishes; and shaded forest lots stay damp long after the rain stops. Winter storms funnelling down Howe Sound add wind exposure that mainland sea-level neighbourhoods rarely see.

{COMPANY_NAME} serves the whole island — village lots in Snug Cove, waterfront properties, and acreages on the quiet west side — alongside nearby communities like Lions Bay, West Vancouver, and Gibsons across the Sound. Our {BUSINESS_TYPE} team brings {YEARS_IN_BUSINESS} years of experience and {SERVICE_LIST} to every {PROPERTY_TYPE}, backed by {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP}.

Islanders talk, and reputations here are earned one job at a time. If you want {SERVICE} from a provider that plans for the crossing instead of complaining about it, request your {ESTIMATE_TYPE} or call {PHONE} today.
Local_Facts:
- Population: 4,256 (2021 Census, Statistics Canada)
- The only island municipality in British Columbia, incorporated in 1999
- Located in Howe Sound, about 19 km from downtown Vancouver
- Reached by BC Ferries from Horseshoe Bay to Snug Cove, roughly a 20-minute crossing
- Mount Gardner is the island's highest point, with Crippen Regional Park and Killarney Lake near Snug Cove
- Historically a Union Steamship Company resort destination in the early 1900s
- Mild oceanic climate with heavy November-March rainfall driving moss, drainage, and moisture issues on building exteriors
